title: Add refunds endpoint and customer transaction history
summary: This PR introduces two new endpoints and a refunds service but has several blockers before it can merge. Security-hawk is alarmed by a `card_number` field being serialized into the customer history response, which is a direct PCI-DSS violation; all `console.log` calls also bypass the project's pino redaction allowlist. Perf-skeptic blocks on a load-all-then-filter-in-memory pattern on the hot customer-history path and an N×M scan of the global refunds store per transaction. Quality-critic finds a silent correctness bug (sorting on `created_at` while the domain object uses `createdAt` — the sort is always a no-op), a 200 returned for a missing transaction that should be 404, and three tests that literally prove nothing (`toBeDefined()`, `lessThan(500)`). Kiss-zealot flags the module-level `refundsStore` singleton (untestable, leaks across tests) and a speculative dollars-vs-cents guessing block that has no business being in a payments service.

findings.title: card_number field serialized into customer history response — PCI violation
findings.message: Line 43 casts `tx` to `{ card_number?: string }` and emits that field in the JSON response under the key `card_number`. Even if the current domain object doesn't carry a raw PAN, the code is designed to expose it the moment one appears on the transaction. PCI-DSS prohibits returning full card numbers from a server API. Additionally the field is named `card_number` but currently emits the last-4 value — callers can't tell what they're getting. Remove `card_number` entirely and keep only `card_last4` (or the camelCase equivalent). Audit whether any path actually stores a raw PAN on the Transaction object.

findings.title: console.log bypasses pino redaction allowlist
findings.message: Lines 25 and 55 both use `console.log` to emit transaction IDs and refund amounts. The project ships a pino logger specifically configured with a redaction allowlist for PCI compliance; anything written to `console.log` bypasses those controls and goes to stdout in cleartext. Switch to the injected logger: `logger.warn({ transactionId }, 'transaction not found for refund')` and `logger.info({ refundId: refund.refundId, transactionId: tx.id, amount: refund.amount }, 'refund created')`. Same issue in customerHistoryController.ts line 15.

findings.title: console.log leaks customerId to stdout, bypassing pino redaction
findings.message: `customerId` is PII. Writing it to `console.log` skips the project's pino redaction pipeline and emits it in plaintext to any log collector or stdout sink. Use the injected structured logger instead: `logger.info({ customerId }, 'history requested')`. This applies identically to refundsService.ts lines 25 and 55.

findings.title: Module-level refundsStore is hidden global state
findings.message: `const refundsStore: Record<string, RefundResult> = {}` lives at module scope, is never injected, can't be reset between tests (the test suite currently creates a new `createApp()` per `it()` block, but the store is shared across all of them — a refund created in test 1 is visible in test 3), and can never have two instances. The rest of the codebase injects repositories via constructor. Do the same here: create a `RefundRepository` (or reuse the existing pattern) and inject it into `RefundsService`. The fix is mechanical and eliminates the entire class of cross-test leakage.

findings.title: findAll() + in-memory filter — O(all transactions) per customer request
findings.message: Every call to `GET /customers/:id/transactions` fetches the entire transaction table (`findAll()`) and then filters in JavaScript. On 50 seed rows this is imperceptible; on 5 M rows it reads the full table on every request. This endpoint is exactly what `findByCustomer(customerId)` is for — push the predicate to the store (a `WHERE customer_id = $1` with an index). `findAll()` should be removed from the `TransactionRepository` interface; it has no legitimate unbounded-read use case. Current query count: 1 full-table read per request.

findings.title: N×M: enrichTransactionWithRefunds called in a loop, each call scans all refunds
findings.message: `enrichTransactionWithRefunds` is awaited inside a `for...of` loop over the customer's transactions. Inside, it calls `listRefundsForTransaction`, which iterates `Object.keys(refundsStore)` — scanning every refund ever recorded — to find matches for one transaction ID. For a customer with N transactions and M total refunds in the store, this is N×M work per request. Batch it: fetch all refunds for the relevant transaction IDs in one pass (`Object.values(refundsStore).filter(r => txIds.has(r.transactionId))`), group by transactionId, then do the enrichment without a loop of async calls. Query count today: 1 full-scan of refundsStore per transaction.

findings.title: No pagination — endpoint returns unbounded list of transactions
findings.message: A customer with a long history gets every transaction in a single response, with no `limit`/`offset`/cursor parameters and no `Link` header. This is an OOM incident waiting for the right customer account. Add `limit` (default 50, max 200) and a cursor or `offset` parameter. The in-memory implementation can implement this trivially; the interface should reflect the bounded contract from day one.

findings.title: Sort on `created_at` is a silent no-op — domain field is `createdAt`
findings.message: The comparator reads `a.created_at` and `b.created_at`, but `Transaction` domain objects use camelCase `createdAt`. `created_at` is `undefined` on every element, so all comparisons return `0` and the array is never actually sorted — the response always comes back in insertion order, not newest-first. The comment even notes the intent ('sort newest first') but the field name doesn't match. Fix: `(a, b) => b.createdAt.localeCompare(a.createdAt)` (or compare `Date` objects). Also remove the `(a: any, b: any)` cast — TypeScript would have caught this if the types were kept.

findings.title: HTTP 200 returned when transaction is not found — should be 404
findings.message: When `refund()` returns `null` (transaction not found), the controller responds `200 {}`. A 200 with an empty body tells the caller "success, nothing to report" — the opposite of "the resource you referenced doesn't exist." This breaks retry logic, idempotency clients, and any monitoring that relies on status codes. Return `404` with an error body: `res.status(404).json({ error: 'transaction not found' })`. Note: `result == null` should also be `result === null` to match the project's strict-equality convention.

findings.title: All three assertions prove nothing — tests manufacture false confidence
findings.message:
Test 1 asserts `expect(refund.body).toBeDefined()` — this passes if the endpoint returns a 500, a 400, or any garbage response. Test 2 asserts `expect(refund.status).toBeLessThan(500)` — passes with the current broken `200 {}` for a missing transaction, so the test actively validates wrong behavior. Test 3 asserts `expect(res.body).toBeDefined()` — passes for an empty array, a 404, or anything at all. These three tests provide zero regression protection. Replace them with real assertions:
- Test 1: `expect(refund.status).toBe(201)` and `expect(refund.body).toMatchObject({ transactionId: charge.body.id, amount: 1000, status: 'succeeded' })`
- Test 2: `expect(refund.status).toBe(404)`
- Test 3: check `res.status === 200`, `res.body.transactions` is an array, `res.body.count` is a number
Also add: partial refund, over-refund rejection, and invalid-amount tests.

findings.title: Speculative dollars-vs-cents guessing logic is undefined behavior in a payments service
findings.message: The block on lines 37-44 silently converts a refund amount it thinks might be in dollars to cents — based on a guess. This is exactly the kind of heuristic that causes irreversible money errors: a legitimate 1-dollar refund on a 500-cent transaction becomes 100 cents instead. The API contract must define the unit (cents); if a caller sends the wrong unit, reject the request with a 400, don't silently reinterpret it. The amount-capping that follows (`refundAmount = tx.amount`) is also silent — the caller has no idea their requested amount was ignored. Remove the guessing logic, define `amount` as always-cents in the schema, and raise a 422 if `amount > tx.amount`.

findings.title: `as unknown as { card_number?: string }` escapes the type system to reach a field that doesn't exist
findings.message: `Transaction` does not have a `card_number` property — the type assertion is the developer working around the compiler's correct objection. The compiler is right. If the intent is to expose last-4 digits, use `tx.cardLast4` directly. If a raw card number were somehow on the domain object, `@ts-nocheck`-style casts are not the answer — fix the domain type. Delete this line; keep only `card_last4: withRefunds.cardLast4`.

findings.title: amount schema accepts zero, negative, and non-integer values
findings.message: `z.number().optional()` lets through `0`, `-500`, and `9.99` (fractional cents). A refund of zero or a negative amount is nonsensical; fractional cents will cause rounding surprises downstream. Change to `z.number().int().positive().optional()` to enforce the invariant at the boundary, where it belongs, rather than inside business logic.

findings.title: console.log throughout — project uses pino structured logger
findings.message: Lines 25 and 55 use `console.log` while every other service in this codebase uses the injected pino logger. Beyond the redaction issue (filed separately by security_hawk), raw `console.log` doesn't carry log level, request ID, or structured fields, making it useless in production log aggregation. Replace with the injected logger — `RefundsService` should receive a `logger` in its constructor just like the other services.

findings.title: Loose equality `==` instead of `===` — naming convention drift throughout response
findings.message:
Line 22 uses `tx.customerId == customerId` (loose equality); `refundsController.ts` line 20 uses `result == null`. The project lint config enforces `===`. Beyond style, `==` between a stored string ID and a URL param string is accidental — it works but only because both happen to be strings. 

The response object in `enriched` also mixes naming conventions: `card_number`, `card_last4`, `customer_id`, `gateway_reference`, `created_at` (snake_case) alongside `id`, `amount`, `currency`, `status`, `refunds` (camelCase). The rest of the API uses camelCase; this endpoint will force every consumer to special-case it.

findings.title: historyQuerySchema defined but never used — dead code
findings.message: `historyQuerySchema` is exported but not imported anywhere. The customer-ID comes from `req.params.id` in the controller, not from query parameters. Delete this definition; git remembers it if it's ever needed.

findings.title: Manual for-loop where .filter() would do
findings.message: Lines 19-25 implement a manual indexed `for` loop with a push to accumulate filtered transactions. `const mine = all.filter(tx => tx.customerId === customerId)` does the same thing in one line and is idiomatic TypeScript. Minor, but the manual loop obscures intent and is the kind of noise that accumulates.

findings.title: No negative-path tests for refund business rules
findings.message: The tests cover only the most basic happy path and a trivially weak not-found case. Missing tests include: partial refund (amount < tx.amount), over-refund attempt (amount > tx.amount should be rejected with a 422, not silently capped), refunding the same transaction twice, invalid `amount` values (zero, negative, non-integer), and missing `transaction_id`. For money-adjacent endpoints, the negative paths are where bugs live — they need to be proven by tests, not assumed.
